# Topic 4 - Lagrangian Duality

## Introduction

You now have the complete toolkit for understanding unconstrained convex optimization. Norms gave you geometry. Convex sets gave you feasibility. Convex functions gave you tractability. The Legendre-Fenchel transform gave you duality between function values and supporting hyperplanes, between primal descriptions and dual descriptions, between the inside view and the outside view.

But every optimization problem you will encounter in the roadmap has constraints. The Kantorovich problem constrains the transport plan to have fixed marginals. The score matching problem constrains the score function to integrate correctly. The GNN equivariance problem constrains the network weights to satisfy symmetry conditions. The diffusion model constrains the forward and reverse processes to share the same stationary distribution. Constraints are not an inconvenience layered on top of optimization — they are the mathematical expression of the physical, geometric, and probabilistic structure of the problem.

What you need is a systematic framework for handling constraints — one that converts a constrained optimization problem into a form that is easier to analyze, easier to solve, and easier to dualize. That framework is Lagrangian duality.

The core idea is disarmingly simple. When you have a constrained optimization problem — minimize $f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0$ and $h_j(x) = 0$ — you can convert it into an unconstrained problem by adding penalty terms to the objective. Each constraint gets a multiplier — a Lagrange multiplier — and the constraint violation is added to the objective weighted by that multiplier. The resulting unconstrained objective is the **Lagrangian**. Minimizing the Lagrangian over $x$ and then maximizing over the multipliers produces the dual problem — a new optimization problem whose solution bounds the original from below and, under the right conditions, equals it exactly.

This topic has two tightly connected subtopics.

The first subtopic is **primal vs. dual problems** — the Lagrangian construction itself, the definition of the dual function as the infimum of the Lagrangian over the primal variable, the weak duality theorem stating that the dual always lower-bounds the primal, and the strong duality theorem stating that the gap closes to zero under constraint qualifications. The strong duality theorem is the Lagrangian version of the Fenchel duality theorem you proved in the previous topic — they are two faces of the same result, and you will see their connection explicitly. The dual problem is always convex — even when the primal is not — because the dual function is the infimum of affine functions of the multipliers, and infima of affine functions are concave.

The second subtopic is the **KKT conditions** — the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker first-order necessary and sufficient conditions for optimality in constrained convex optimization. The KKT conditions are a system of four requirements that a primal-dual pair $(x^*, \lambda^*, \nu^*)$ must satisfy at optimality: primal feasibility, dual feasibility, stationarity of the Lagrangian, and complementary slackness. Complementary slackness — the condition that $\lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0$ for every inequality constraint — is the Lagrangian version of the Fenchel equality condition from the previous topic. It says that at optimality, either a constraint is active — $g_i(x^*) = 0$ — or its multiplier is zero — $\lambda_i^* = 0$ — and never both simultaneously active and non-zero unless the problem is degenerate. Complementary slackness is the mechanism by which the Lagrangian duality framework identifies which constraints actually matter at the optimum and which are irrelevant.

Together, these two subtopics complete Chapter 2's engine. The Legendre-Fenchel transform built the duality machinery for unconstrained problems. Lagrangian duality extends that machinery to constrained problems — which is every problem in the roadmap. The KKT conditions are the operational output: a system of equations and inequalities that characterizes the optimal solution completely, and that every numerical algorithm for constrained convex optimization is ultimately solving, whether explicitly or implicitly.

By the end of this topic, when you see the Kantorovich dual written as a supremum over potential functions, you will recognize it as a Lagrangian dual — the Lagrange multipliers of the marginal constraints of the transport problem are exactly the potential functions $\phi$ and $\psi$. The KKT conditions of the transport problem are exactly the complementary slackness conditions that say: a pair $(x,y)$ is in the support of the optimal transport plan if and only if $\phi(x) + \psi(y) = c(x,y)$ — the transport cost is exactly exhausted by the potentials. The Lagrangian framework is not a new tool sitting beside the Legendre-Fenchel transform — it is the same duality, applied to the constrained setting, completing the picture.

---
---
